title: Leydig Cell Hypoplasia (LCH): Two Published Cases of LHCGR Mutations in 46,XY DSD
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: early childhood LCH Type 1 (Çömlek 2021) vs complete 46,XY pseudohermaphroditism with V144F receptor ER retention (Richter-Unruh 2004) — hCG stimulation failure, inguinal gonad discovery, and when to suspect Leydig cell aplasia in the DSD workup
category: Pediatric Endocrinology / Disorders of Sex Development
authors: Resident training module — two published case reports (see sourceFile)
sourceFile: Çömlek FÖ et al. Oxford Medical Case Reports 2021 doi:10.1093/omcr/omab015; Richter-Unruh A et al. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2004;89:5161-5167 doi:10.1210/jc.2004-0298

patient.chiefComplaint: Disorders of sex development — two published Leydig cell hypoplasia cases
pmh: Case 1 (Çömlek 2021): 46,XY, predominantly female phenotype, early childhood inguinal swelling — LCH Type 1 with inactivating LHCGR mutation

Case 2 (Richter-Unruh 2004): 46,XY infant of consanguineous parents, clitoral enlargement and labial synechia at birth; incarcerated right testis at 4 months during inguinal hernia repair; undetectable testosterone with no hCG response; homozygous LHCGR V144F

caseDescription: Two peer-reviewed case reports illustrating Leydig cell hypoplasia (LCH) — a rare autosomal recessive 46,XY DSD caused by inactivating mutations in the luteinizing hormone/chorionic gonadotropin receptor (LHCGR/LHR) gene. Impaired LH/CG binding or signal transduction prevents fetal and postnatal Leydig cells from producing testosterone needed for male sexual differentiation. Case 1 (Çömlek et al. 2021): early childhood diagnosis — atypical because most LCH presents at adolescence with primary amenorrhea or delayed puberty; 46,XY patient with predominantly female phenotype identified by inguinal swelling; confirmed inactivating LHCGR mutation (LCH Type 1). Case 2 (Richter-Unruh et al. 2004): classic complete male pseudohermaphroditism — consanguineous pedigree, clitoromegaly and labial synechia at birth, incarcerated testis discovered at 4 months, undetectable testosterone failing to rise after hCG; novel homozygous missense V144F in the extracellular ligand-binding domain; in vitro proof that mutant receptors are retained in the endoplasmic reticulum and never reach the cell surface.
keyFindings: LCH = autosomal recessive LHCGR mutations → Leydig cell failure → undervirilized 46,XY external genitalia
hCG stimulation test: undetectable baseline testosterone with no rise — distinguishes severe Leydig failure from biosynthetic defects with partial reserve
Most cases present at puberty, but inguinal swelling/hernia repair can unmask cryptorchid testes in early childhood
V144F demonstrates mechanism: defective trafficking to cell surface → complete LH/CG unresponsiveness
Consider LCH in any 46,XY DSD with palpable gonads (testes), female external phenotype, and absent androgen response to hCG
finalDiagnosis: Case 1: Leydig cell hypoplasia Type 1 — inactivating LHCGR mutation, diagnosed in early childhood (Çömlek et al. 2021). Case 2: Complete Leydig cell hypoplasia / 46,XY male pseudohermaphroditism — homozygous LHCGR V144F with absent cell surface receptor expression (Richter-Unruh et al. 2004). Teaching synthesis: LCH sits in the 46,XY DSD taxonomy as impaired Leydig cell development/function (LH receptor defect), distinct from androgen biosynthetic enzyme defects and from complete androgen insensitivity syndrome.
learningObjectives: Place Leydig cell hypoplasia within the 46,XY DSD differential (pe-cr-009 framework)
Recognize early childhood and neonatal presentations beyond the classic adolescent delayed puberty
Interpret the hCG stimulation test when testosterone is undetectable and non-stimulable
Explain how LHCGR mutations impair LH/CG signal transduction at the receptor level
Contrast LCH with CAIS and androgen biosynthetic defects using anatomy and hormone patterns

items.title: Why Leydig cell hypoplasia belongs in the 46,XY DSD differential
items.content:
Leydig cell hypoplasia (LCH) is a rare autosomal recessive disorder interfering with normal development of male external genitalia in 46,XY individuals. Within the pe-cr-009 taxonomy, 46,XY DSD arises from impaired testis development, androgen biosynthesis, or androgen action. LCH is a distinct pathway: testes form and Sertoli cells can produce AMH (Müllerian regression may be intact), but Leydig cells cannot respond adequately to LH/hCG through a defective LHCGR — so testosterone and DHT fail to virilize external genitalia.

Pediatricians and endocrinologists must consider LCH whenever a 46,XY child has female or ambiguous external genitalia with palpable gonads and absent androgen effect — not only at adolescence when delayed puberty declares itself.
keyPoints: 46,XY DSD — Leydig failure, not always biosynthesis or AR defect.
Testes present; LH receptor signaling impaired.
Think LCH in undervirilized 46,XY with gonads.

items.title: How LHCGR mutations prevent fetal and postnatal testosterone production
items.content:
The LHCGR (LHR) gene encodes the LH/chorionic gonadotropin receptor on Leydig cells. LH (postnatally) and hCG (fetal life) bind this receptor to drive testosterone synthesis. Inactivating mutations impair ligand binding or downstream signal transduction — fetal Leydig cells cannot produce the testosterone needed for Wolffian stabilization and external virilization (DHT).

Postnatally, the same defect causes undetectable or very low testosterone, absent pubertal virilization, and failure to respond to exogenous hCG stimulation. Severity correlates with residual receptor function — complete loss (Type 1) yields female external phenotype; partial loss (Type 2) may allow mild virilization.
keyPoints: LHCGR = LH/hCG receptor on Leydig cells.
Fetal hCG and postnatal LH drive testosterone.
Complete loss → female external phenotype in 46,XY.

items.title: Case 1: Early childhood LCH Type 1 — inguinal swelling in a 46,XY child
items.content:
Çömlek et al. (2021), Oxford Medical Case Reports. Patient with 46,XY karyotype but predominantly female phenotype — identified early due to swelling in the inguinal region (not the classic adolescent presentation). Diagnostics confirmed an inactivating mutation in the LHCGR gene (Leydig cell hypoplasia Type 1).

Clinical significance: most LCH historically presents during adolescence with primary amenorrhea or delayed puberty; this case underscores that early childhood presentation is possible and that inguinal swelling may be the entry point to diagnosis. Ref: doi:10.1093/omcr/omab015.

items.title: Case 2: Complete 46,XY pseudohermaphroditism — incarcerated testis at 4 months
items.content:
Richter-Unruh et al. (2004), J Clin Endocrinol Metab. Infant born to consanguineous parents: at birth, clitoral enlargement and labial synechia. At four months, surgery for right-sided inguinal hernia revealed an incarcerated right gonad (testis). Endocrine evaluation: undetectable serum testosterone that failed to rise after hCG administration.

Genetics: novel homozygous missense mutation V144F in the extracellular ligand-binding domain of LHCGR. Ref: doi:10.1210/jc.2004-0298; cited 51 times — landmark mechanistic case.
keyPoints: 4 months: incarcerated testis; testosterone undetectable, hCG non-responsive.

items.title: How the V144F LHR mutation causes complete LH unresponsiveness
items.content:
Richter-Unruh et al. modeled V144F in HEK 293 cells and proved the mutated receptors were retained in the endoplasmic reticulum and failed to migrate to the cell surface. Consequently, Leydig cells were completely unresponsive to LH despite circulating gonadotropins — explaining total failure of testosterone production and complete 46,XY male pseudohermaphroditism.

Pearl: not all LHCGR mutations simply reduce binding affinity; trafficking defects abolish surface expression entirely. Functional studies distinguish qualitative signaling defects from quantitative partial loss.
keyPoints: ER retention — no cell surface receptor.

items.title: How the hCG stimulation test distinguishes LCH from androgen biosynthetic defects
items.content:
In 46,XY DSD with female or ambiguous genitalia, short-course hCG stimulation assesses Leydig cell androgenic capacity (pe-cr-009). In LCH, baseline testosterone is undetectable or very low and does not rise after hCG — because Leydig cells cannot transduce the LH/CG signal regardless of stimulus dose.

By contrast, some biosynthetic defects (e.g. partial 17β-HSD3 deficiency) may show incremental testosterone rise with accumulated precursors; 5α-reductase deficiency shows testosterone rise with low DHT. LCH Case 2: testosterone remained undetectable after hCG — pointing to receptor-level Leydig failure, not enzyme block downstream.
keyPoints: LCH: no testosterone rise after hCG.

items.title: Why most LCH presents at puberty but early diagnosis is possible
items.content:
Historical series emphasize adolescent presentation — primary amenorrhea in individuals reared female, or delayed/incomplete puberty in those reared male — because undervirilized external genitalia at birth may not trigger full DSD workup if not severely ambiguous. Case 1 (Çömlek) shows an alternative entry: inguinal swelling leading to gonad discovery in early childhood.

Case 2 shows neonatal ambiguity (clitoromegaly, labial synechia) with definitive diagnosis accelerated by inguinal hernia surgery at 4 months. Any 46,XY infant with inguinal mass, labial fusion, or micropenis/clitoromegaly merits karyotype, electrolytes, testosterone/AMH, pelvic ultrasound, and hCG stimulation per DSD protocol.
keyPoints: Early triggers: inguinal swelling, hernia repair.
Neonatal ambiguity should prompt full DSD workup.

items.title: How to distinguish LCH from CAIS and androgen biosynthetic defects
items.content:
Complete androgen insensitivity (CAIS): 46,XY, female external genitalia, testes often in inguinal canal/labia, normal Leydig testosterone production, elevated LH, high testosterone and estradiol (from aromatisation), negative hCG response is NOT the pattern — testosterone is produced. AR gene mutation.

17β-HSD3 or 17α-hydroxylase deficiencies: impaired testosterone synthesis with characteristic steroid precursor patterns; hCG may raise androstenedione more than testosterone (17β-HSD3). LCH: testes present, often undervirilized female phenotype, undetectable testosterone, absent hCG response, LHCGR sequencing diagnostic. Consanguinity increases likelihood of homozygous LHCGR mutations (Case 2).
keyPoints: Biosynthetic defects: precursor patterns, partial hCG responses.
LCH: no testosterone, no hCG rise, LHCGR mutation.

items.title: How to approach management and counseling in 46,XY LCH
items.content:
Management is multidisciplinary per DSD standards: karyotype confirmation, LHCGR sequencing, imaging of internal structures, discussion of sex of rearing (often female when presentation is complete female phenotype in infancy), gonadal tumor risk surveillance (intra-abdominal or dysgenetic gonads), and psychosocial support.

Pubertal induction requires exogenous testosterone (or DHT where appropriate) — endogenous Leydig response to LH will not occur. Gonadectomy timing is individualized; dysgenetic or abdominal testes carry malignancy risk. Fertility is absent with complete Leydig failure. Genetic counseling for autosomal recessive inheritance — critical in consanguineous families (Case 2). Link to pe-cr-009 for Prader/EMS scoring and gonadectomy principles.

items.title: Two-case comparison: early childhood vs neonatal complete LCH
items.content:
Case 1 (Çömlek 2021): predominantly female 46,XY, inguinal swelling, early childhood diagnosis, inactivating LHCGR — LCH Type 1. Case 2 (Richter-Unruh 2004): consanguineous, clitoromegaly/labial synechia at birth, incarcerated testis at 4 months, undetectable non-stimulable testosterone, homozygous V144F with ER retention and absent surface expression.

Shared lesson: Leydig cell hypoplasia is a 46,XY DSD caused by LHCGR mutations impairing LH/CG signal transduction — not androgen receptor resistance and not steroidogenic enzyme deficiency. hCG non-response plus palpable testes in an undervirilized 46,XY patient should prompt LHCGR sequencing.
keyPoints: hCG failure is the biochemical hallmark.

items.question: Why did testosterone fail to rise after hCG administration in Case 2?
items.answer: Because Leydig cells lacked functional LH/CG receptors on the cell surface. The homozygous V144F LHCGR mutation caused receptor retention in the endoplasmic reticulum, so hCG — which acts through the same receptor — could not initiate testosterone synthesis. This is receptor-level unresponsiveness, not a downstream biosynthetic block that might show partial precursor accumulation with stimulation.
keyPoints: hCG acts via LHCGR on Leydig cells.

items.question: How should inguinal swelling in a 46,XY child with female phenotype be worked up for DSD?
items.answer: Palpate for gonads; obtain karyotype if not already known; measure testosterone, LH, FSH, AMH, and electrolytes; perform pelvic/inguinal ultrasound; execute short-course hCG stimulation to assess Leydig capacity. If testosterone is undetectable and does not rise with hCG, suspect Leydig cell hypoplasia and sequence LHCGR. Inguinal swelling may represent hernia with incarcerated testis (as in Case 2) or lymphadenopathy — imaging and surgical exploration may be diagnostic. Early workup prevents delayed diagnosis until adolescence.

items.question: Why is LCH not the same as complete androgen insensitivity syndrome despite similar female external phenotype?
items.answer: In CAIS, Leydig cells produce normal or elevated testosterone but peripheral tissues cannot respond because of androgen receptor mutations — LH is often elevated and testosterone is not undetectable. In LCH, the defect is upstream at the Leydig LH/CG receptor — testosterone production itself is absent or minimal and does not increase with hCG. Gonadal histology shows Leydig cell hypoplasia/aplasia rather than normal hormone-producing testes with resistant end organs. Genetic testing targets LHCGR in LCH and AR in CAIS.
keyPoints: LCH: testosterone not produced, LHCGR defective.
hCG response and gene testing discriminate.

items.question: How did Richter-Unruh et al. prove that V144F caused receptor dysfunction rather than reduced binding alone?
items.answer: They expressed wild-type and V144F LHR in HEK 293 cells and demonstrated that mutant receptors were retained in the endoplasmic reticulum and did not traffic to the plasma membrane. Without cell surface expression, ligand binding cannot occur regardless of affinity — cells were completely unresponsive to LH. This trafficking defect explained the clinical phenotype of total testosterone failure and complete 46,XY male pseudohermaphroditism more fully than a simple hypomorphic binding mutation.
keyPoints: ER retention — no plasma membrane receptor.
Complete functional loss, not partial binding.

items.stem: Leydig cell hypoplasia is most commonly inherited as:
items.explanation: LCH from LHCGR mutations follows autosomal recessive inheritance — homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations; consanguinity increases risk (Case 2).

items.stem: In Richter-Unruh et al. (2004), the identified LHCGR mutation was:
options: Homozygous V144F in the extracellular ligand-binding domain
items.explanation: Novel homozygous missense V144F in the LHR extracellular domain — consanguineous pedigree.

items.stem: A 46,XY infant with female external genitalia has undetectable testosterone that does not rise after hCG stimulation. The most likely category of diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Absent testosterone production with no hCG response indicates Leydig failure at the LH receptor level — LCH. CAIS typically has testosterone production; 5α-reductase shows testosterone rise with low DHT.

options: It was diagnosed in early childhood rather than at adolescence
It involved a 46,XX karyotype with virilization
The mutation was in the androgen receptor gene
items.explanation: Most LCH presents at puberty; Case 1 was identified early via inguinal swelling with LHCGR mutation.

items.stem: The V144F LHCGR mutation causes hormone resistance primarily by:
options: Increasing aromatase activity in Sertoli cells
Preventing receptor trafficking to the cell surface
items.explanation: Mutant receptors retained in ER — fail to reach plasma membrane → complete LH/CG unresponsiveness.

items.stem: Within the 46,XY DSD framework (pe-cr-009), Leydig cell hypoplasia is best classified as:
options: Impaired Leydig cell response to LH/hCG (LHCGR defect)
items.explanation: LCH is Leydig cell hypoplasia/aplasia from LHCGR mutations — distinct from AR defects or biosynthetic enzyme blocks.

items.stem: At birth, the infant in Case 2 (Richter-Unruh) presented with:
items.explanation: Complete undervirilization — clitoromegaly and labial synechia at birth; testis found at hernia surgery at 4 months.

items.stem: The gene mutated in Leydig cell hypoplasia encodes:
options: The LH/chorionic gonadotropin receptor on Leydig cells
items.explanation: LHCGR (LHR) — LH/CG receptor on Leydig cells; inactivating mutations cause LCH.

items.statement: Leydig cell hypoplasia from LHCGR mutations is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
items.explanation: Homozygous/compound heterozygous LHCGR mutations; consanguinity a risk factor.

items.statement: In complete Leydig cell hypoplasia, testosterone typically rises robustly after hCG stimulation.
items.explanation: Case 2: undetectable testosterone failed to rise after hCG — hallmark of Leydig receptor failure.

items.statement: Leydig cell hypoplasia can only be diagnosed after puberty has failed to occur.
items.explanation: Case 1 diagnosed in early childhood; Case 2 had neonatal ambiguity and diagnosis by 4 months.

items.statement: The V144F LHCGR mutation can cause receptor retention in the endoplasmic reticulum with absent cell surface expression.
items.explanation: Proven in HEK 293 cells by Richter-Unruh et al. — complete LH unresponsiveness.

items.statement: Complete androgen insensitivity syndrome and Leydig cell hypoplasia both typically show undetectable testosterone with no hCG response.
items.explanation: CAIS has testosterone production (often elevated) with AR resistance; LCH has absent testosterone production.

items.assertion: Leydig cell hypoplasia should be considered in the differential of 46,XY DSD with female or ambiguous external genitalia and palpable gonads.
items.reason: Inactivating LHCGR mutations prevent Leydig cells from producing testosterone in response to fetal hCG and postnatal LH, causing undervirilization despite testicular tissue.
items.explanation: Both true — core teaching point linking anatomy, hormones, and genetics.

items.assertion: Homozygous V144F in the LHCGR gene can explain complete 46,XY male pseudohermaphroditism in Case 2.
items.reason: The mutation causes receptor retention in the endoplasmic reticulum so that Leydig cells lack functional LH/CG receptors on the cell surface.
items.explanation: Both true — genotype-phenotype mechanism proven in vitro.
